edge: Machine learning models are scaled in both sequence length and model dimension, while existing architectures like Transformers scale quadratically along both axes.
Introduces a new architecture, MONARCH MIXER (M2), that uses Monarch matrices to scale sub-quadratically along both sequence length and model dimension.
M2 matches BERT-base and BERT-large in quality with fewer parameters and higher throughput at sequence length 4K in the domain of non-causal BERT-style modeling.
M2 outperforms ViT-b by 1% in accuracy on ImageNet with only half the parameters.

Enforcing causality via masking introduces a quadratic bottleneck in causal GPT-style models, which is alleviated by developing a novel theoretical view of Monarch matrices based on multivariate polynomial evaluation and interpolation.
M2 matches GPT-style Transformers at 360M parameters in pretraining perplexity on The PILE, showing the potential to match Transformer quality without attention or MLPs.

node_2: natural language processing and computer vision
edge: Machine learning models in natural language processing and computer vision are being stretched to longer sequences and higher-dimensional representations to enable longer context and higher quality.
Existing architectures exhibit time and space complexities that grow quadratically in sequence length and/or model dimension.
Attention and MLP in Transformers scale quadratically in sequence length and model dimension.
Monarch matrices are a simple, expressive, and hardware-efficient class of sub-quadratic structured matrices.
sequence dimension and model dimension are key terms mentioned in the context that play a significant role in the discussion of machine learning models.
MLP-mixer and ConvMixer are machine learning models that operate by mixing information along the sequence and model dimension axes. They use a single operator for both axes.
MLP-mixer uses MLPs while convolutions are used in ConvMixer. Both models are expressive but scale quadratically in their input dimension.
Sub-quadratic sequence mixing and long convolutions are approaches proposed for mixing information in sequences. Both methods aim to reduce the computational complexity compared to quadratic scaling.

Dense MLP layers and Monarch matrices are types of matrices used in machine learning models. Monarch matrices are proposed as sub-quadratic structured matrices to enhance the performance of the model.
MONARCH MIXER (M2) uses Monarch matrices to mix information along the sequence and model dimension axes.
Monarch matrices generalize the fast Fourier transform (FFT).
Monarch matrices have been shown to capture a wide class of linear transforms including Hadamard transforms.
Monarch matrices have been shown to capture Toeplitz matrices.
Monarch matrices have been shown to capture AFDF matrices.
Monarch matrices have been shown to capture convolutions.
Monarch matrices are parameterized as the products of block-diagonal matrices, called monarch factors.
Monarch factors are interleaved with permutation.
Monarch factors can be computed efficiently on modern hardware using matrix multiplication.
Monarch factors can be computed efficiently on modern hardware using GEMMs.
M2 uses Monarch matrices to mix information along the sequence.
M2 uses Monarch matrices to mix information along the model dimension axes.
Our proof-of-concept implementation of an M2 layer, written in less than 40 lines of pure PyTorch, achieves 25.6% FLOP utilization for inputs of size 64K on an A100 GPU.
M2 layer implementation in PyTorch relies only on matrix multiplication.
M2 layer implementation in PyTorch relies only on transpose.
M2 layer implementation in PyTorch relies only on reshape.
M2 layer implementation in PyTorch relies only on elementwise products.
On an A100 GPU, our proof-of-concept implementation of an M2 layer achieves 25.6% FLOP utilization, whereas a simple CUDA implementation on RTX 4090 achieves 41.4% FLOP utilization at the same size.
On an A100 GPU, our proof-of-concept implementation of an M2 layer achieves 25.6% FLOP utilization for inputs of size 64K.
On RTX 4090, a simple CUDA implementation achieves 41.4% FLOP utilization at the same size.

GPU is a key component behind the cost of deep learning operations on GPUs.
GPU has a relevant factor affecting the runtime performance of deep learning operations.
Operations can be classified as compute-bound or memory-bound based on the balance between computation and memory accesses.
Compute-bound operations such as matrix multiply with large inner dimension have a relatively small time accessing GPU memory compared to arithmetic operations.
The speed of operations utilizing tensor cores is determined by the FLOP/s available on compute units.
Tensor cores can achieve 312 TFLOP/s in half-precision matrix multiply operations on the A100 GPU.
Tensor cores are limited to 19 TFLOP/s in non-matrix multiply operations on the A100 GPU.
The trend of using tensor cores started with V100 chips [58], and is now moving towards the next-generation H100 chips [60].
In memory-bound operations, the time taken by the operation is determined by the number of memory accesses.
In memory-bound operations, the time spent in computation is much smaller compared to the time taken by memory accesses.
The runtime of memory-bound operations is determined by the memory bandwidth of different layers of the memory hierarchy, where GPU memory provides large storage but relatively slow bandwidth.
Higher levels of the memory hierarchy such as caches are much smaller in size (20 MB) but significantly faster in bandwidth (19 TB/s).
